description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Oauth client secret leak and possible IDOR leading to PII Disclosure

tech_stack_fingerprints: Javascript files containing environment variables on login/recovery pages
API endpoints returning PII or user lists

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an OAuth login or username recovery page reveals environment variables or credentials in Javascript files, and associated API endpoints are discoverable.
prerequisites: Access to an OAuth login page of the target SaaS product.
Ability to generate an access token for an authenticated user.
applicability_signals: Javascript files associated with OAuth login/recovery pages containing what appear to be environment variables or credentials.
Discovery of API endpoints that accept site-specific ID values.
API endpoints that, when tested with an authenticated access token, return PII.
skip_if: No sensitive information found in Javascript files on OAuth pages.
API endpoints are not discoverable or do not accept user-specific IDs.
API responses do not contain PII when tested with an authenticated token.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse OAuth login pages (e.g., oauth.example.com) and related username recovery pages. Inspect the source code and linked Javascript files for environment variables, API endpoints, or other secrets.
Step 2: Use tools like Gau to discover API endpoints associated with api.example.com, especially those that take site-specific ID values.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to generate an access token using credentials or information found in the Javascript file.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite Intruder or a similar tool to iterate through discovered site-specific ID values against relevant API endpoints (e.g., /widgets endpoint, /me endpoint, or similar).
Step 3: Analyze API responses for any signs of PII disclosure or access to data belonging to other users.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /oauth/generate_token HTTP/1.1
Host: oauth.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

client_id=<client_id>&client_secret=<client_secret>&grant_type=password&username=<username>&password=<password>

GET /api/v1/users/<ID_VALUE> HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Authorization: Bearer <ACCESS_TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests, using Intruder)
Gau (for discovering subdomains and endpoints)
curl (for crafting custom requests)
success_indicators: API responses containing Personally Identifiable Information (PII) of other users (Full Names, Usernames, Email Addresses, Phone Numbers, Employee IDs, Job Titles, Login Timestamps).
Responses indicating access to data beyond the authenticated user's permissions, such as lists of users for an organization.
failure_indicators: API responses return 401 Unauthorized, indicating the access token is invalid or insufficient.
API responses return 404 Not Found for all ID values, suggesting the IDOR is not present or requires different enumeration.
API responses return generic error messages or the same data for all ID values, indicating no IDOR.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive PII for customers or users of the SaaS platform, potentially leading to account compromise, social engineering, or other privacy violations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Inspect Javascript files on OAuth and login-related pages for leaked credentials or API interaction details, and always test ID parameters in API endpoints for IDOR vulnerabilities using authenticated tokens.

meta.title: Pwn2Own Vancouver 2021 :: Microsoft Exchange Server Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: An unauthenticated attacker in a privileged network position (e.g., Man-in-the-Middle) can trigger RCE when an administrative user runs the `Update-ExchangeHelp` or `Update-ExchangeHelp -Force` command.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to perform a Man-in-the-Middle (MiTM) attack on the target network.
applicability_signals: Target is Microsoft Exchange Server.
The `Update-ExchangeHelp` or `Update-ExchangeHelp -Force` command is executed by an administrative user.
skip_if: The target environment prevents MiTM attacks (e.g., strong network segmentation, TLS inspection).
The `Update-ExchangeHelp` command is not available or not used.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Microsoft Exchange Server instances within the network.
Step 2: Confirm administrative users have the ability to run `Update-ExchangeHelp` or `Update-ExchangeHelp -Force`.
Step 3: Establish a Man-in-the-Middle (MiTM) position within the network.
attack_steps: Step 1: As an attacker in a MiTM position, intercept the network traffic related to the Exchange Server.

Step 2: When an administrative user executes `Update-ExchangeHelp` or `Update-ExchangeHelp -Force`, manipulate the `ManifestUrl` parameter configured in the registry (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\ExchangeServer\v15\UpdateExchangeHelp\ManifestUrl`) to point to a malicious URL controlled by the attacker.
Step 3: The Exchange Server will attempt to download a manifest from the attacker-controlled URL.
Step 4: The attacker-controlled server serves a malicious manifest file which, when processed by the Exchange Server, leads to Remote Code Execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious manifest file content: insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for MiTM interception and response manipulation)
Ettercap or similar MiTM tools
Custom HTTP server to serve malicious manifest file
success_indicators: Remote Code Execution on the Microsoft Exchange Server.
failure_indicators: Exchange Server logs an error indicating failure to download the manifest or process it. → Verify MiTM setup and the attacker-controlled URL accessibility. Check if the `ManifestUrl` registry key is correctly set and if the Exchange process has permissions to access it.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker in a privileged network position can achieve Remote Code Execution on the Microsoft Exchange Server, potentially leading to full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always consider the security implications of Man-in-the-Middle (MiTM) attacks, especially in environments where administrative actions involve downloading external resources based on potentially mutable configurations like registry values.

meta.title: Business Logic Ratings Bug

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML contains star rating UI elements
Backend request sends integer rating value

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows users to submit ratings on a scale (e.g., 1-5 stars) and the backend does not properly validate these inputs.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target application.
The target application must have a user-facing rating submission feature.
applicability_signals: The rating submission uses a UI element like a star selector.
Interception of the submission request reveals an integer parameter for the rating.
The application has been observed to not throw errors for unexpected rating values during manual testing.
skip_if: All rating submission requests consistently return errors for non-standard rating values (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 500 Internal Server Error for values other than 1-5).
The rating parameter is not exposed directly in the request and is heavily obfuscated or handled client-side only without a backend component to manipulate.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the target application and identify any features related to user reviews or ratings.
Step 2: Submit a rating using the provided UI elements (e.g., star selection) and intercept the HTTP request using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 3: Analyze the intercepted request to identify the parameter responsible for storing the rating value and observe its data type (expected to be an integer).
attack_steps: Step 1: With the intercepted rating submission request from Step 2 of Recon, modify the integer rating parameter to a value outside the expected range (e.g., -5000, 5000).
Step 2: Forward the modified request to the server.

Step 3: Observe the server's response. If a 500 error occurs, try progressively smaller negative numbers or larger positive numbers until a successful submission is observed (as per writeup, -500 worked).
Step 4: If the modified rating is accepted, submit ratings with extreme positive and negative values to maximize the impact on the average rating calculation.
Step 5: Re-visit the target location's profile or search results page to observe if the average rating has been skewed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Repeater or Intruder tool to systematically test a range of integer values for the rating parameter.
Web browser developer tools: For initial request interception and analysis.
success_indicators: The server accepts requests with rating values outside the expected range (e.g., 1-5).
The average rating displayed for a location is drastically altered (e.g., goes significantly above 5 or below 1).
A 200 OK response is received when submitting an out-of-range rating.
failure_indicators: All attempts to submit ratings outside the 1-5 range result in server errors (e.g., 4xx or 5xx status codes).
The application UI or backend logic prevents submission of non-standard integer values.
After submitting an out-of-range rating, the displayed average rating remains unchanged.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can manipulate the average rating of any location on the platform by submitting extreme positive or negative ratings, thereby artificially inflating or deflating its perceived popularity. This can lead to misleading users, impacting businesses negatively or unfairly promoting certain locations, and ultimately eroding user trust in the platform's rating system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate all user-supplied input parameters, especially numeric ones, on the server-side to ensure they fall within acceptable business logic constraints before processing.

meta.title: Retrieve Archived Stories Of Any Public Instagram Account

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target API endpoint `api/v1/ads/graphql` is found to be exposed and accessible by authenticated users.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session token for Instagram.
applicability_signals: Discovery of the `api/v1/ads/graphql` endpoint.
The endpoint accepts `id` parameter within `query_params`.
skip_if: API requests to `api/v1/ads/graphql` are consistently rejected with authentication errors, even with a valid session.
The `id` parameter is not present or is obfuscated in the request.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Instagram API endpoint `api/v1/ads/graphql` through network traffic analysis or known API documentation.
Step 2: Analyze the parameters accepted by the `api/v1/ads/graphql` endpoint, specifically looking for user identifiers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `/api/v1/ads/graphql` with the following parameters: `surface=story_grid`, `doc_id=3271888199508091`, `locale=en_GB`, `vc_policy=insights_policy`, `signed_body=SIGNATURE.`, `strip_nulls=true`, `strip_defaults=true`, and `query_params` containing `{"count":15,"cursor":"0","timeframe":"LIFETIME","searchBase":"USER","promoteEligibility":"ELIGIBLE","trackingCondition":"CREATED_BEFORE_TRACKING_INCLUDED","is_user":"true","queryParams":{"access_token":"<SESSION_TOKEN>","id":"[YOUR_USERID]"}}`
Step 2: Replace `[YOUR_USERID]` with your own Instagram user ID to confirm the request structure and retrieve your own archived stories (if any).
Step 3: Obtain the user ID of a target Instagram user.
Step 4: Re-send the request from Step 1, replacing `[YOUR_USERID]` with the target's user ID in the `query_params.queryParams.id` field.
Step 5: Analyze the response for archived stories belonging to the target user.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /api/v1/ads/graphql HTTP/1.1
Host: instagram.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

surface=story_grid&doc_id=3271888199508091&locale=en_GB&vc_policy=insights_policy&signed_body=SIGNATURE.&strip_nulls=true&strip_defaults=true&query_params=%7B%22count%22%3A15%2C%22cursor%22%3A%220%22%2C%22timeframe%22%3A%22LIFETIME%22%2C%22searchBase%22%3A%22USER%22%2C%22promoteEligibility%22%3A%22ELIGIBLE%22%2C%22trackingCondition%22%3A%22CREATED_BEFORE_TRACKING_INCLUDED%22%2C%22is_user%22%3A%22true%22%2C%22queryParams%22%3A%7B%22access_token%22%3A%22<SESSION_TOKEN>%22%2C%22id%22%3A%22<TARGET_USER_ID>%22%7D%7D
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify API requests.
Instagram API documentation or proxies: To identify the specific GraphQL endpoint and its parameters.
success_indicators: Response contains archived stories data (e.g., image URLs, timestamps) belonging to the target user.
HTTP status code 200 OK.
failure_indicators: HTTP status code 400 Bad Request or 403 Forbidden indicates a malformed request or insufficient permissions.
Response contains an error message indicating the user ID is invalid or the stories are not accessible.

If the `id` parameter is not the sole identifier for fetching user-specific data, further enumeration may be required. Pivot to looking for other user-identifying parameters within the `query_params` object.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can retrieve all archived stories of any public Instagram user, potentially exposing private or sensitive content that the user intended to keep archived and private.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check if user identifiers within nested JSON or query parameters of API requests can be manipulated to access data belonging to other users, especially for endpoints related to user-specific content retrieval.

meta.title: Vulnerability in Bumble dating app reveals any user's exact location

tech_stack_fingerprints: Official Bumble app behavior (showing distance to other users)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application displays the distance between the authenticated user and other users. This technique is applicable if the application's backend sends precise distance data that can be used for trilateration.
prerequisites: An authenticated user account on Bumble.
applicability_signals: The Bumble app displays distances to other users (e.g., '5 miles away').
The application's network traffic reveals distance data with high precision.
skip_if: The application only displays rounded distances (e.g., '5 miles') and does not send precise decimal values in network requests.
The application's network traffic does not reveal any distance data between users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Install the Bumble mobile application and create an authenticated user account.
Step 2: Use a network proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to intercept and analyze the network traffic generated by the Bumble app.
Step 3: Observe the API requests and responses related to user matching and distance calculations.
attack_steps: Step 1: While authenticated in the Bumble app, note the reported distance to a target user (or a known user if available).
Step 2: Intercept the network request that fetches distance information for a specific user.
Step 3: Analyze the response to find the precise distance value (e.g., 5.21398760815170 miles).

Step 4: Repeat steps 1-3 for multiple target users, ensuring these users are in different known locations relative to the attacker's device. (This requires moving the attacker's device or having control over multiple target accounts).
Step 5: Collect at least three precise distance measurements to distinct locations.

Step 6: Use a trilateration algorithm or tool with the collected precise distances and the known locations of the target users to calculate the attacker's approximate location, which corresponds to the victim's location if the distance is between the attacker and the victim.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a passive observation and analysis technique, not requiring specific crafted payloads for the vulnerable endpoint itself, but rather observation of existing data).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing network traffic.
Trilateration calculator/library: For performing the location calculation using precise distance data.
success_indicators: Successful calculation of the target user's approximate geographical location via trilateration.
The calculated location is consistent with the known or inferred location of the target user.
failure_indicators: The application only provides rounded distance values (e.g., '5 miles') and does not expose precise decimal values in the response.
Network traffic analysis reveals no distance data between users or only coarse-grained location information.
Trilateration yields inconsistent or wildly inaccurate results, indicating the underlying distance data is not precise enough for this method.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can determine the exact location of any Bumble user by observing the precise distance data returned by the application and applying trilateration techniques. This could lead to stalking, doxxing, or physical harassment.
playbooks.key_lesson: When applications reveal distances between users, always check if the distance data is sent with high precision. If it is, investigate the possibility of using trilateration to reconstruct the user's location.

meta.title: How i was able to steal private files of any user on Larksuite

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: Applicable when the target application allows users to upload files and assigns a unique token to each file, and when this token is predictable or can be reused for other users' files.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account on Larksuite.
applicability_signals: Observe the POST request made after uploading an image in the 'Footer' feature.
Identify a 'file token' parameter in the request or response.
skip_if: If file tokens are cryptographically random and not guessable.
If the application properly enforces access control based on file ownership, not just the token.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Larksuite as a regular user and explore the file upload functionality, specifically the 'Footer' feature.
Step 2: Intercept requests made during the file upload process using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 3: Identify the file token assigned to the uploaded file in the request or response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upload a file (e.g., an image) to the footer feature and capture the request containing the file token.
Step 2: Analyze the captured request to understand how the file token is generated or represented.
Step 3: Attempt to guess or enumerate other valid file tokens. If other users' files have predictable tokens, this step is crucial.
Step 4: Craft a new request, replacing the original file token with a potentially valid token belonging to another user's private file.
Step 5: Send the tampered request to the server.
Step 6: If the server responds with a broken image or allows viewing of the file when 'view image' is clicked, the private file has been accessed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and replay HTTP requests. Use Intruder for token enumeration if applicable.
success_indicators: A broken image is displayed on clicking 'view image'.
The private file of another user is downloaded without explicit permission.
The response from the server indicates successful retrieval of the file content associated with the guessed token.
failure_indicators: A 403 Forbidden or similar access denied error is received.
The application returns an invalid file token error.
A generic error page is displayed without any file content.
If failure occurs, consider if tokens are truly guessable or if there's another mechanism to access files. Pivot to exploring other features for IDOR vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized access to and download private files uploaded by any user on Larksuite, regardless of their permissions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test file upload functionality for IDOR by attempting to access files using tokens or IDs belonging to other users, especially after observing predictable patterns in token generation.

meta.title: By Design: How Default Permissions on Microsoft Power Apps Exposed Millions

affected_components: Power Apps portals with OData APIs configured for public access

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Microsoft Power Apps portal's OData API is configured to allow public access, potentially exposing sensitive data alongside public information.
prerequisites: The target application is built using Microsoft Power Apps.
The Power Apps portal has an OData API that is accessible.
applicability_signals: Product documentation describes OData APIs can be made publicly accessible.
Power Apps marketing page lists access to 'your data either anonymously or through commercial authentication' as a feature.
skip_if: The Power Apps portal strictly enforces authentication for all data access.
WAF blocks requests to OData endpoints.

recon_steps: Identify Microsoft Power Apps portals in scope.
Investigate Power Apps portals for OData API endpoints that may be accessible anonymously. Look for API patterns often associated with data retrieval (e.g., /api/data/v9.1/entitynames).
attack_steps: Attempt to access OData API endpoints directly using various HTTP methods (GET, POST).
If anonymous access is permitted, analyze the response for sensitive data like personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, or authentication credentials.
If specific entities are discovered, attempt to enumerate other related entities or fields by modifying API calls.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite/OWASP ZAP: Intercept and modify requests to OData endpoints. Use the Intruder for fuzzing parameters or enumerating entities.
Custom scripts (Python, PowerShell): Automate the discovery and querying of OData APIs.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response containing sensitive information that should not be publicly accessible.
Successful enumeration of entities or fields not intended for public consumption.
Discovery of PII, employee data, vaccination details, or other sensitive personal records.
failure_indicators: Receiving 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden responses for all OData API requests, indicating proper authentication is enforced.
Receiving empty or non-sensitive data in responses.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive personal information, including names, email addresses, social security numbers, employee IDs, and COVID-19 related data, to unauthenticated attackers. This can lead to identity theft, fraud, and privacy violations for millions of individuals.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test Microsoft Power Apps portals for publicly accessible OData APIs and scrutinize responses for any sensitive data that should be protected by authentication and authorization.

meta.title: Story Of Unexpected Bugs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an unsubscribe link in an email contains a hexadecimal encoded email parameter, and the application uses this parameter to process the unsubscribe request.
prerequisites: Attacker must have received an email from the target with an unsubscribe link.
applicability_signals: Presence of an 'unsubscribe' link in an email.
The unsubscribe link contains a 'email' parameter with a hexadecimal encoded value.
The target URL uses a cgi-bin path.
skip_if: The 'email' parameter in the unsubscribe link is not hexadecimal encoded.
The 'email' parameter is not present in the unsubscribe link.

recon_steps: Step 1: Receive an email from the target and inspect the unsubscribe link for parameters.
Step 2: Identify if the 'email' parameter is present and if its value is hexadecimal encoded.
attack_steps: Step 1: Decode the hexadecimal value of the 'email' parameter from the unsubscribe link to reveal the recipient's email address.
Step 2: Craft a new hexadecimal encoded email address (e.g., a fake email or an XSS payload encoded).
Step 3: Replace the original hexadecimal encoded email value with the crafted one in the unsubscribe URL.
Step 4: Submit the modified unsubscribe URL.
payloads_or_patterns: Hex encoded payload template: <script>alert(document.domain)</script> -> Convert to uppercase -> Encode to Hexadecimal
tools_recommended: CyberChef (for Hexadecimal decoding and encoding)
Burp Suite (to intercept and modify requests)
success_indicators: IDOR: Successfully unsubscribing another user from emails without their consent (observable by receiving emails that should have been unsubscribed).
XSS: Execution of the JavaScript payload, e.g., an alert box appearing with the domain name.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error response (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 500 Internal Server Error) indicating an invalid email format or parameter. -> Suggest retrying with a different encoding or carefully checking the target's expected format.
The unsubscribe link does not contain the 'email' parameter or it's not hexadecimal encoded. -> Move to a different vulnerability type.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can unsubscribe any user from the target's email notifications, leading to potential denial of service for legitimate communication. Attacker can also execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session if the unsubscribe page renders untrusted content.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test hexadecimal encoded parameters, especially email addresses, by attempting to inject different valid email addresses or crafted payloads after decoding and re-encoding.

meta.title: MonkeyType.com Stored Cross-Site Scripting

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can post messages to a chat room that is rendered by other users.
prerequisites: User must be authenticated.
applicability_signals: Presence of a chat feature accessible to authenticated users.
Messages posted in the chat are displayed to other users.
skip_if: Chat messages are heavily sanitized and do not allow any HTML or script tags.
Chat messages are not displayed to other users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to monkeytype.com.
Step 2: Navigate to the 'Tribe Chat' feature to confirm its availability and observe message rendering.
Step 3: Attempt to post simple HTML tags (e.g., test) to see if they are rendered or stripped.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a JavaScript payload designed to execute in the context of other users viewing the chat.
Step 2: Post the crafted payload as a message in the Tribe Chat.
Step 3: Have another authenticated user view the chat to observe the execution of the payload.
Step 4: Inspect the browser's developer console and network requests for signs of XSS execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying chat messages.
Browser developer tools for observing JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: An alert box appears on the screen of another user viewing the chat.
Arbitrary JavaScript code executes in the browser of other users, as observed in the developer console.
failure_indicators: The posted script tags are displayed as plain text or are stripped entirely. Try more sophisticated XSS payloads or encoding techniques.
No JavaScript execution is observed. The vulnerability might be patched or requires specific user interaction not yet identified.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browsers of other users interacting with the tribe chat. This can lead to session hijacking, data theft (e.g., sensitive information displayed in the chat), defacement of the chat interface, or redirecting users to malicious websites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-generated content, especially in chat features, for stored XSS vulnerabilities by injecting various HTML and JavaScript payloads.

meta.title: Playing With s3 Leaks

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target organization is identified to be using AWS S3 buckets and has subdomains that might be related to these buckets.
prerequisites: The target organization uses AWS S3.
Subdomains for the target organization are known or can be enumerated.
applicability_signals: Subdomains ending in `.s3.amazonaws.com` or appearing in `waybackurls` output related to S3.
Response from `ffuf` indicating accessible S3 buckets.
skip_if: No S3 related endpoints or subdomains are found.
All enumerated S3 buckets return 'Access Denied' or 403 errors.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains for the target domain using `subfinder -d <TARGET_DOMAIN> > subdomains.txt`.
Step 2: Fuzz potential S3 bucket names by iterating through enumerated subdomains. Use `ffuf -u http://FUZZ.s3.amazonaws.com -w subdomains.txt` to identify accessible buckets.
Step 3: Corroborate potential S3 buckets by checking historical URLs. Use `cat subdomains.txt | waybackurls | grep s3.amazonaws.com` to find S3 related URLs.
attack_steps: Step 1: For each potentially identified S3 bucket (e.g., `sub.redacted.com.s3.amazonaws.com`), attempt to list its contents using the AWS CLI: `aws s3 ls s3://<BUCKET_NAME>`.
Step 2: If `aws s3 ls` is successful and lists objects, then the bucket is misconfigured and publicly accessible. Navigate to the objects if possible to confirm sensitive data leakage.
tools_recommended: subfinder: For subdomain enumeration.
ffuf: For fuzzing and discovering S3 buckets.
waybackurls: For historical URL analysis.
awscli: For interacting with S3 buckets (e.g., `aws s3 ls`).
success_indicators: `aws s3 ls s3://<BUCKET_NAME>` command successfully lists objects within the bucket.
Accessing a direct URL to an object in the bucket (e.g., `http://<BUCKET_NAME>/<OBJECT_KEY>`) returns the object content without authentication.
failure_indicators: `aws s3 ls s3://<BUCKET_NAME>` returns 'Access Denied' or 'NoSuchBucket'. Pivot by trying different subdomain enumeration techniques or fuzzing patterns.
ffuf responses are consistently 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found for all fuzzing attempts. This may indicate a more sophisticated WAF or lack of S3 usage associated with the subdomains.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive data stored in public S3 buckets, which can include user data, private pictures, bills, identity cards, and other confidential information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Enumerate subdomains diligently and use tools like ffuf and waybackurls to discover potential S3 buckets. Always attempt to list bucket contents using `aws s3 ls` to confirm public accessibility and identify leaked data.

meta.title: Timing Attack on SQL Queries Through Lobste.rs Password Reset

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses Ruby on Rails (inferred from code snippets)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses a predictable format for password reset tokens that includes a timestamp, and the database query for the token can be observed for timing differences.
prerequisites: The target application must have a password reset feature.
The password reset token must contain a timestamp or a predictable element that influences the query execution time.
The application must be susceptible to timing attacks, meaning that variations in query execution time can be measured.
applicability_signals: Password reset tokens start with a numeric string followed by a hyphen.
The backend code parses a timestamp from the token.
A direct SQL query is performed using the full password reset token.
The timing of the response to a password reset token validation request can be measured.
skip_if: Password reset tokens are purely random strings with no predictable elements.
The password reset token validation is a fast, constant-time operation.
The application uses a prepared statement or parameterized query that effectively prevents timing attacks on the token value itself.

recon_steps: Step 1: Request a password reset for a known user account to obtain a password reset token.
Step 2: Analyze the format of the received password reset token. Look for patterns, especially leading numeric sequences or timestamps.
Step 3: If a timestamp is suspected, attempt to decode it to understand its format and potential range.
attack_steps: Step 1: For each possible timestamp value within a reasonable range (e.g., a few days before and after the current time), construct a potential password reset token by combining the timestamp with a random string suffix (if the format requires it).
Step 2: Send a request to the password reset validation endpoint with each constructed token.
Step 3: Measure the response time for each request.
Step 4: Identify tokens that result in significantly faster response times compared to others. These faster responses likely indicate a valid token and therefore a valid timestamp.

Step 5: Once a valid timestamp is identified, attempt to enumerate the random string part of the token to find the exact token for a specific user, or brute-force user accounts using the identified timestamp pattern.
payloads_or_patterns: For example, if the current time is August 20, 2021 10:00 AM UTC, try timestamps around 1629415200.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: Configure Intruder to send requests with varying timestamp prefixes and measure response times.
Custom scripting (Python, Ruby): To automate the generation of tokens and timing analysis.
success_indicators: A significant and consistent difference in response times between valid and invalid password reset tokens.
A response indicating a successful password reset or password change confirmation for a target account when a specific token is used.
The ability to predict or determine parts of a password reset token based on timing.
failure_indicators: All password reset token validation requests take a similar amount of time, indicating no discernible timing differences.
The application returns a generic error or timeout for all requests, preventing timing analysis.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks requests that appear to be part of a brute-force or enumeration attempt.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of any user whose password reset token can be guessed or enumerated through timing attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Ensure that password reset tokens are not directly usable in database queries in a way that reveals timing information. Use unique, unpredictable tokens and perform validation in constant time.

Step 2: Analyze the response for the presence of a WhatsApp number, even if the user's privacy settings indicate it should be hidden.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /api/v1/users/<USER_ID>/info/ HTTP/1.1
Host: i.instagram.com

Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially POST requests to the Instagram API, and to analyze responses.

meta.title: A New Attack Surface on MS Exchange Part 2 - ProxyOracle!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can be tricked into visiting a malicious link, and the system uses Form-Based Authentication with cookies for session management.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session or can trick a victim into clicking a link.
Target is using Microsoft Exchange with Outlook Web Access (OWA).
applicability_signals: Presence of OWA login page.
Observation of cookies related to session management.
System processes user identity serialization/deserialization.
skip_if: Target does not use OWA.
Authentication mechanism does not involve cookies for session state.
WAF blocks XSS attempts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access OWA login page for the target domain to confirm its presence.
Step 2: Observe HTTP requests and responses related to OWA login and session management, looking for cookies and headers like X-CommonAccessToken.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious link that, when visited by an authenticated user, triggers a Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (CVE-2021-31195) vulnerability.
Step 2: The XSS payload should inject JavaScript capable of capturing the User Identity string from the X-CommonAccessToken header.
Step 3: Transmit the captured User Identity string to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 4: Use the captured User Identity string to perform a Padding Oracle Attack (CVE-2021-31196) on Exchange Cookies Parsing.
Step 5: Exploit the padding oracle to decrypt or manipulate the cookie, potentially recovering the user's plaintext password.
payloads_or_patterns: Padding Oracle Attack on Exchange Cookies Parsing payload requires precise knowledge of the cookie structure and padding scheme, which is not explicitly detailed in the writeup for a generic payload template.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) for intercepting and analyzing HTTP traffic.
Custom script for orchestrating the XSS delivery and the subsequent padding oracle attack.
Collaborator (e.g., Burp Collaborator) for receiving out-of-band data.
success_indicators: Successful execution of the XSS payload, confirmed by a request to the attacker-controlled server containing the X-CommonAccessToken.
Successful decryption or manipulation of the cookie via the padding oracle attack.
Recovery of the victim's plaintext password.
failure_indicators: XSS payload does not execute or does not capture the token (e.g., due to sanitization or WAF). -> Investigate other XSS vectors or different attack surfaces.
Padding Oracle Attack fails to decrypt the cookie (e.g., due to incorrect padding oracle implementation or cookie format). -> Re-verify cookie parsing and padding scheme details.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can recover the plaintext password of an authenticated user by tricking them into visiting a malicious link, leading to account compromise and potential further privilege escalation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate cookie parsing mechanisms for padding oracle vulnerabilities, especially when combined with XSS that can leak sensitive token information from HTTP headers.

Step 3: Exploit CVE-2021-34523 to elevate privileges or execute commands within the PowerShell backend.

meta.title: How to Hack Apple ID

Parameters like `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `response_type`, `state`, and `authVersion` in authorization requests.

Step 2: Examine the parameters sent during the authorization request, looking for `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, and `state`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious `redirect_uri` that points to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 2: Initiate the OAuth 2.0 flow by navigating to the authorization endpoint with the malicious `redirect_uri` and a valid `client_id` and `state`.
Step 3: Upon successful authorization and redirection to the attacker's server, capture the authorization code.
Step 4: Exchange the authorization code with the token endpoint (if applicable) for access tokens or session identifiers.
Step 5: Use the obtained tokens to impersonate the user and access their account or sensitive information.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator for capturing callbacks
Custom scripts for constructing OAuth requests and handling responses
success_indicators: Receipt of an authorization code or token on the attacker-controlled callback server.
Successful subsequent API calls using the stolen credentials or tokens.
failure_indicators: The authorization server rejects the request due to an invalid or disallowed `redirect_uri`. Pivot: Try to find other vulnerabilities like open redirects on the main application that could lead to the OAuth flow.
The authorization server does not redirect to the attacker's callback URL, indicating potential validation or lack of a vulnerable flow.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of the user's Apple ID, leading to unauthorized access to all services linked to it (e.g., iCloud, App Store purchases, device data).
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with OAuth 2.0 flows, strictly validate all redirect URIs server-side to prevent attackers from hijacking the authentication process.

meta.title: Two weeks of securing Samsung devices: Part 2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application exposes unsafe content providers that can be accessed by other applications or by crafting specific intents.
prerequisites: The target device must have the vulnerable Samsung application installed.
applicability_signals: The application handles URIs that can be controlled by the user.
The application exposes content providers with potentially insecure export settings.
Proof of concept code demonstrating interaction with content providers is available.
skip_if: The content provider requires specific authentication or permissions that cannot be bypassed.
The application does not handle user-controlled URIs in a way that could lead to file access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify installed Samsung applications and their package names on the target device.
Step 2: Analyze application manifests (AndroidManifest.xml) for exported Content Providers.
Step 3: Investigate how these Content Providers handle incoming URIs and data, looking for opportunities to manipulate file paths or access sensitive data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an Intent targeting the vulnerable activity (e.g., SmsViewerActivity in Samsung Messages).

Step 2: Populate the Intent's extras with a crafted SmsViewerData object, setting the `f25878w` field to a malicious URI pointing to a sensitive file (e.g., content://com.samsung.android.messaging.ui.file/root-path/data/data/com.samsung.android.messaging/databases/message_content.db).
Step 3: Set a desired output filename in the `f25879x` field.
Step 4: Start the activity.
Step 5: After a delay, retrieve the content of the dumped file using a separate mechanism (e.g., the `dumpFile` method shown in the PoC which uses `MediaStore.Files.getContentUri` to read the file).
payloads_or_patterns: Intent payload for Samsung Messages: `setClassName("com.samsung.android.messaging", "com.samsung.android.messaging.ui.view.viewer.SmsViewerActivity"); i.putExtra("xms_viewer_data", data);` where `data` is an `SmsViewerData` object with `f25878w = Uri.parse("content://com.samsung.android.messaging.ui.file/root-path/data/data/com.samsung.android.messaging/databases/message_content.db")` and `f25879x` set to a desired filename.
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (ADB) for installing apps and interacting with the device.
Java Development Kit (JDK) for compiling and running Android applications/proofs of concept.
Android Studio for debugging and development.
success_indicators: The specified file (e.g., message_content.db) is successfully written to the application's cache directory (e.g., /sdcard/Android/data/com.samsung.android.messaging/cache/).
The content of the sensitive file is retrievable and readable via subsequent ContentResolver operations.
Log output confirms successful file reading or errors during file access.
failure_indicators: The target activity crashes or fails to start.
The application throws an exception when trying to access the file.
The dumped file is empty or contains unexpected data.
A 403 Forbidden error or similar permission denied response is received when attempting to read the file.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can read sensitive files from the filesystem of the target application, potentially including databases containing user messages, call logs, contacts, or other private information, by exploiting vulnerable Content Providers and application logic.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize URIs passed to Content Providers and ensure that sensitive files are not exposed through insecure mechanisms, especially when user input is involved in constructing these URIs.

meta.title: CVE-2021-22929 – Brave Browser 1.27 and below permanently logs the server connection time for all v2 tor domains to ~/.config/BraveSoftware /Brave-Browser/tor/data/tor.log

Step 2: Read the contents of the log file to identify connection timestamps for v2 onion domains.
tools_recommended: Standard OS file access commands (e.g., `cat`, `less`, `more` on Linux/macOS, or File Explorer on Windows).

meta.title: A Bug's Life: CVE-2021-21225

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target environment utilizes the V8 JavaScript engine and the `Array.prototype.concat` method is invoked with specific configurations that trigger a race condition or state machine anomaly.
prerequisites: Target must be running a version of Google Chrome or another product utilizing the vulnerable V8 engine version.
The JavaScript code must call `Array.prototype.concat` in a way that interacts with the complex state machine of the method, potentially involving prototypes, getters, or array-like objects.
applicability_signals: Analysis of the V8 engine's source code for `Array.prototype.concat` reveals specific state transitions or edge cases.
The presence of `Symbol.isConcatSpreadable` or other special properties on array-like objects being concatenated.
Use of `Object.defineProperty` to make TypedArray elements configurable.
skip_if: The V8 engine version is patched against CVE-2021-21225.
The JavaScript execution environment does not support the specific features or behaviors of `Array.prototype.concat` that are exploited.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target's V8 JavaScript engine version.

Step 2: Review the source code of `Array.prototype.concat` in the specific V8 version to understand its state machine and potential weaknesses, particularly those related to TypedArrays and their configurability.
Step 3: Analyze the provided historical vulnerabilities (CVE-2016-1646, CVE-2017-5030) to understand how similar issues in `Array.prototype.concat` were triggered.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct JavaScript code that leverages the `configurable: true` property on TypedArray elements as described in the writeup.

Step 2: Create a scenario where `Array.prototype.concat` is called with carefully crafted arguments that interact with the internal state machine, potentially including array-like objects with getters that modify state during concatenation.
Step 3: Trigger the specific sequence of operations that leads to the out-of-bounds read, exploiting the race condition or state mismanagement.
Step 4: If the out-of-bounds read is confirmed, proceed with advanced V8 exploitation techniques (as described in Part 2 of the writeup) to achieve code execution.
tools_recommended: Chrome DevTools: For debugging JavaScript execution and inspecting V8 engine state.
V8 Debugger (e.g., using LLDB): For in-depth analysis of memory corruption and exploitation.
Custom JavaScript fuzzing frameworks: To explore the state space of `Array.prototype.concat`.
success_indicators: Crashing the renderer process with a specific memory corruption signature indicative of an out-of-bounds read.
Achieving arbitrary code execution within the renderer process, confirmed by executing a known shellcode payload.
Observing successful execution of techniques like disabling JIT W^X or leaking pointer compression isolated root values.
failure_indicators: No crash observed, indicating the specific conditions for the vulnerability were not met.
A generic crash not related to out-of-bounds memory access.
The exploit payload does not achieve code execution after triggering the initial memory corruption.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution within the Google Chrome renderer process, leading to potential further compromise of the user's system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Complex JavaScript engine state machines, like those in `Array.prototype.concat`, are fertile ground for vulnerabilities. Researchers should analyze how language specification changes interact with existing engine implementations to uncover subtle bugs.

meta.title: Exploiting CVE-2021-21225 and disabling W^X
meta.authors: Brendon T i s z k a

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When analyzing or testing the V8 JavaScript engine, specifically when dealing with array manipulations in versions susceptible to CVE-2021-21225.
prerequisites: Target environment must be running a vulnerable version of the V8 JavaScript engine (e.g., version 9.0.257).
Ability to execute JavaScript code within the V8 engine (e.g., via d8 or a browser using a vulnerable V8 version).
applicability_signals: The presence of code paths for HOLEY_DOUBLE_ELEMENTS, PACKED_DOUBLE_ELEMENTS, HOLEY_ELEMENTS, or PACKED_ELEMENTS array types.
The use of `Array.prototype.concat` in conjunction with these array types.
skip_if: The V8 engine version is patched against CVE-2021-21225.
The execution environment does not allow for JavaScript code execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of the V8 JavaScript engine being used. This may involve checking browser version strings or specific application components.
Step 2: If a vulnerable version is identified, examine JavaScript code for patterns involving `Array.prototype.concat` and array element handling.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft JavaScript code to create a HOLEY/PACKED_DOUBLE_ELEMENTS array and trigger the out-of-bounds read using `elements->get_scalar(j)` by controlling `j`.
Step 2: Observe the values read into a TypedArray returned by `Symbol.species` to infer information such as the JSArray's map pointer, properties pointer, length, and elements pointer.
Step 3: (For code execution) Craft JavaScript code to create a HOLEY/PACKED_ELEMENTS array and trigger the out-of-bounds read using `elements->get(j)` to obtain a pointer to a JSObject.
Step 4: Leverage the leaked pointers (from Step 2 and 3) to bypass security mitigations like W^X (Write XOR Execute) and potentially achieve arbitrary code execution within the V8 sandbox.
payloads_or_patterns: // Code to trigger out-of-bounds read for information leak
// Code to trigger out-of-bounds read for object pointer leak
// Code to exploit leaked pointers for RCE
tools_recommended: d8 (V8's command-line shell) for executing JavaScript exploits.
rr (Record and Replay) for debugging and analyzing execution flow.
GDB with gef for debugging and memory inspection.
Bash scripting for orchestrating the exploit execution with rr and GDB.
success_indicators: Successful information leak of internal V8 engine memory addresses (e.g., JSArray map, elements, properties pointers).
Demonstration of disabling W^X protection.
Successful execution of arbitrary code within the V8 engine sandbox.
failure_indicators: No abnormal memory reads or data corruption observed when executing the exploit payload.
V8 engine crashes with segmentation faults or other unhandled exceptions without yielding meaningful information.
W^X protection remains active.
Suggestion: If information leak fails, try different array types or carefully construct the array to control its state.

playbooks.impact: Enables attackers to leak sensitive internal memory addresses of the V8 JavaScript engine, which can be a stepping stone to bypassing security mitigations like W^X and achieving arbitrary code execution within the browser or other applications utilizing V8.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing JavaScript engines, pay close attention to array manipulation primitives and how out-of-bounds reads can lead to information disclosure and subsequent exploitation of security mitigations.

meta.title: Why u should use burp to test Path Traversal Vulnerability and also get RXSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can submit a numerical ID to an endpoint that is vulnerable to path traversal, and there is a functionality to preview email templates that can embed URLs.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to the target application.
An endpoint exists that accepts a numerical ID and is vulnerable to path traversal.
A feature exists that allows previewing email templates where URLs can be embedded.
applicability_signals: Endpoint `/material/profile/endpoint/{numerical_id}` is present.
Appending `../../..` to the numerical ID in Burp Suite yields a directory listing response, while in the browser it redirects to the main domain.
An internal endpoint for formal email templates is identified.
A 'preview' functionality for email templates is available.
skip_if: Path traversal attempts do not result in directory listing or unexpected responses in Burp Suite.
The email template preview functionality does not allow embedding custom URLs.
A WAF blocks path traversal attempts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify an endpoint that accepts a numerical ID, such as `/material/profile/endpoint/{numerical_id}`.

Step 2: Use Burp Suite to send requests to this endpoint with various path traversal payloads (e.g., `../../..`, `../../../etc/passwd`) appended to the numerical ID. Observe the response in Burp Suite and compare it to the response seen when loading the URL directly in the browser.

Step 3: If Burp Suite shows a directory listing or unexpected internal files while the browser redirects, confirm path traversal vulnerability. If not, try fuzzing the numerical ID for other vulnerabilities.
Step 4: Browse through discovered internal endpoints to find features related to email template management or sending emails.
Step 5: Locate an email template preview functionality and check if custom URLs can be embedded.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a path traversal payload to reach a desired directory, e.g., by appending `../../..` to the numerical ID.
Step 2: Identify an endpoint responsible for email template preview, e.g., an internal API endpoint for formal emails.
Step 3: Inject a simple XSS payload into the email template and use the 'preview' functionality to confirm it renders as a Self-XSS (e.g., an alert box).

Step 4: Craft a CSRF proof-of-concept (PoC) that triggers the email template preview functionality with the XSS payload. This might involve intercepting the preview request and replaying it via a crafted HTML form.
Step 5: Create a 'test email' using a template that accepts a URL parameter. Insert the RXSS URL (the CSRF PoC that triggers the XSS) into this parameter.
Step 6: Send this test email to an administrator or any user who might open it. When the recipient clicks the link in the email, their cookies will be captured via the RXSS vulnerability.
payloads_or_patterns: Path Traversal: `/material/profile/endpoint/1/../../..` (assuming `1` is a valid ID and 4 levels up is sufficient)
RXSS URL for email: `<TARGET_URL>/path/to/email/template/preview?xss_param=<XSS_PAYLOAD_HERE>` (this is a hypothetical structure for illustration, actual RXSS payload generation is needed)
CSRF POC structure for RXSS: Simple HTML form that submits a POST request to the email template preview endpoint with the necessary parameters including the XSS payload.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting requests, modifying parameters, directory brute-forcing, and observing detailed responses.
Burp Collaborator (or similar): For detecting blind XSS or SSRF callbacks.
Custom script: To take screenshots of URLs for visual verification.
success_indicators: Response 200 with directory listing contents when path traversal payload is appended in Burp.
An alert box appears when previewing an email template with a simple XSS payload.
The recipient's cookies are captured on the attacker's server after clicking a link within a test email.
failure_indicators: 404 or 403 errors for all path traversal attempts: Indicates WAF or proper input validation. Pivot to other vulnerabilities.
XSS payload doesn't execute in preview: Template sanitization or lack of rendering. Check other templates or functionalities.
No cookie capture after recipient clicks link: RXSS payload is not correct, or the email is not opened/clicked. Verify RXSS payload and test email delivery mechanism.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve Remote Cross-Site Scripting (RXSS) by tricking an authenticated user (potentially an admin) into clicking a link within a test email. This can lead to session hijacking, data theft, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test path traversal vulnerabilities in conjunction with other features like email templates or file uploads, as they can be chained to achieve more severe impacts like RXSS or RCE.

meta.title: Simple HTML Injection to $250

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user (e.g., a manager) has the ability to edit user profiles, and the email change functionality is vulnerable to business logic flaws.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session in the target application.
Attacker must be able to access the user profile editing functionality.
applicability_signals: Email input field on user profile is visually disabled but might be present in the request.
Requests to update user profiles return a 200 OK status code even when attempting to change a disabled field like email.
Application logic does not strictly validate the email address against existing user accounts during password reset.
skip_if: Email change requests consistently return an error (e.g., 400, 403, 500).
Password reset functionality strictly validates that the entered email belongs to an existing user and rejects unknown addresses.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the target application with an authenticated user account (e.g., manager).
Step 2: Navigate to the user management section and identify a target employee's profile.
Step 3: Observe the user profile page, noting how the email address field is presented (e.g., disabled, grayed out).
Step 4: Use browser developer tools or a proxy (like Burp Suite) to intercept the request when attempting to save changes to the user profile.
Step 5: Analyze the intercepted request to identify parameters related to user profile updates.
attack_steps: Step 1: Forward the intercepted request to modify the user profile, but add an `email` parameter to the request body with the attacker's desired email address (e.g., `ahmd_halabi+test@wearehackerone.com`).
Step 2: Send the modified request and observe the response. A 200 OK status code is an indicator of potential success, even if the response body shows the original email.
Step 3: Log out of the attacker's account.

Step 4: Attempt to log in using the target employee's original credentials. A failure with an error like 'Information provided does not match our records' indicates the account is no longer accessible with the old email.
Step 5: Initiate the 'Reset Password' functionality for the target employee's account, using the target employee's original email address.

Step 6: If a password reset email is successfully triggered for the employee's original email (even if the attacker changed the email on record), this confirms the vulnerability and allows the attacker to reset the password and take over the account.
payloads_or_patterns: Request Body: `"email":"<ATTACKER_EMAIL>"` (sent along with other profile update parameters)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Browser Developer Tools: For observing network requests and responses.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK status code when attempting to change the email address via a POST request with an added `email` parameter.
Target employee's credentials failing to log in after the email change attempt.
Successful initiation of a password reset email to the target employee's original email address after the email change attempt.
Receiving the password reset email at the target employee's email address and successfully resetting the password to gain account access.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error response (e.g., 400, 403, 500) when attempting to add the `email` parameter.
Password reset request for the employee's email returns an error like 'We don't have that email address on file'.
Successful login with employee credentials after attempting the email change.

playbooks.impact: Complete Account Takeover of employee accounts without their knowledge, allowing the attacker to access sensitive information, perform unauthorized actions, or frame the employee.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the server-side handling of all parameters, even those that appear disabled or protected on the client-side. Business logic flaws in profile updates can lead to account takeover via password reset mechanisms.

affected_components: Deserialization of YAML input using SnakeYAML without SafeConstructor

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application deserializes YAML input using a vulnerable version of the SnakeYAML library without proper configuration (e.g., not using SafeConstructor).
prerequisites: The target application must be using a Java-based YAML deserialization library that is vulnerable to arbitrary code execution via deserialization.
applicability_signals: The application uses the Kubernetes Java Client.
The application deserializes YAML data from an untrusted source.
The SnakeYAML library is used without the SafeConstructor configuration.
skip_if: The SnakeYAML library is configured with SafeConstructor.
The application does not deserialize YAML input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application uses the Kubernetes Java Client by reviewing project dependencies or code.
Step 2: Determine if the application deserializes YAML input from any source, especially user-controlled input.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious YAML payload designed to exploit SnakeYAML's unsafe deserialization feature.
Step 2: Inject the crafted YAML payload into an input point where the application deserializes YAML.
Step 3: Observe for signs of arbitrary code execution on the target system.
tools_recommended: Custom Java exploit JAR (e.g., from https://github.com/jordyv/poc-snakeyaml) hosted on an attacker-controlled HTTP server.
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP to intercept and modify requests containing YAML input.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary code on the target server, such as establishing a reverse shell.
Network callback from the target server to the attacker-controlled server (e.g., when the `poc.jar` is downloaded and executed).
failure_indicators: The application handles the YAML input without error but does not execute arbitrary code (likely due to SafeConstructor being used).
Errors related to YAML parsing or instantiation of Java classes if the payload is malformed or the Java environment is restricted.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server where the vulnerable Kubernetes Java Client or SnakeYAML deserialization occurs, allowing an attacker to control the affected system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When deserializing YAML input, always use the SafeConstructor configuration in SnakeYAML to prevent arbitrary code execution.

meta.title: Access to CrowdTangle Deletion Framework API

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When interacting with the CrowdTangle API, especially if a GraphQL endpoint is identified.
prerequisites: An authenticated session token for a CrowdTangle user.
applicability_signals: Presence of a GraphQL endpoint that responds to queries.
Queries related to deletions or content management.
skip_if: GraphQL endpoint returns authorization errors for all queries.
No GraphQL endpoint is discoverable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the GraphQL endpoint for the CrowdTangle API. This might be a common endpoint like /graphql or discoverable through API documentation.
Step 2: Attempt to query the GraphQL endpoint with a valid authenticated session to confirm its functionality and response structure.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the GraphQL endpoint with a query designed to retrieve deletion-related information. Use a valid <SESSION_TOKEN>.
Step 2: Analyze the response for data points like `__typename`, `id`, `parent_id`, and `system`. Pay close attention to the `parent_id`.
Step 3: Cross-reference `parent_id` values with publicly available information (e.g., Google search, cached pages) to identify associated Facebook pages or entities.
Step 4: Attempt to enumerate or systematically query for deletion results over a period of time to gather a substantial dataset.
payloads_or_patterns: Mutation/Query for deletions: {"query": "{ deletedObjects { __typename id parent_id system } }"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify GraphQL requests, and to analyze responses.
GraphQL client (e.g., GraphiQL, Insomnia): To construct and send GraphQL queries.
success_indicators: Response containing a list of deleted objects with `__typename`, `id`, `parent_id`, and `system` fields.
Successful correlation of `parent_id` with known Facebook pages or accounts.
Discovery of sensitive `parent_id` values that should not be accessible.
failure_indicators: GraphQL endpoint returns authorization errors (e.g., 401, 403) for all deletion-related queries.
The query returns no relevant data or only empty lists.
No identifiable `parent_id` or `system` fields in the response.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain access to sensitive information about deleted Facebook objects (posts, pages) including their IDs and potentially associated account owners. This could be used for further reconnaissance or to identify patterns of content deletion.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test GraphQL endpoints for improper authorization, especially when dealing with sensitive data like deletion logs or user activity.

meta.title: View the country of a private Instagram User

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access Instagram user data via an API endpoint that is part of the ad tools or a similar administrative function.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Instagram/Facebook session.
applicability_signals: Discovery of an endpoint like `/ads_rights_manager/account_preview_contents/` that accepts an `ig_ent_user_id` parameter.
The target application has features related to advertising or user management that might expose user metadata.
skip_if: The API endpoint is removed or heavily protected against unauthorized access.
All API responses explicitly deny access to private user data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze network traffic for authenticated user actions, particularly those involving user profile data or administrative functions.
Step 2: Identify API calls that take an Instagram User ID (e.g., `ig_ent_user_id`) as a parameter and observe the structure of the response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `/ads_rights_manager/account_preview_contents/` with a valid authenticated session cookie.
Step 2: Include a parameter `ig_ent_user_id` with the ID of a target Instagram user (which can be obtained through other means or brute-forced if numeric).
Step 3: Observe the response JSON for a field named `main_account_location`.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /ads_rights_manager/account_preview_contents/ HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "ig_ent_user_id": "<TARGET_IG_USER_ID>"
}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests to the identified endpoint, fuzzing the `ig_ent_user_id` parameter if necessary.
Browser Developer Tools: Observe network requests and responses during normal application usage to identify potential endpoints.
success_indicators: The JSON response contains the field `main_account_location` with a value representing the country of the target Instagram user, even if the target account is private.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found response: Indicates the endpoint might be restricted or non-existent.
Receiving a response without the `main_account_location` field: Suggests the specific user ID or the endpoint is not vulnerable as expected.
Receiving a generic error: May require further investigation into the exact input or endpoint behavior.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of the country associated with a private Instagram user's account, potentially violating privacy expectations. This information could be used for targeted profiling or further social engineering attempts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate administrative or ad-related API endpoints for potential information disclosure vulnerabilities, as they may expose sensitive user metadata even for private accounts.

meta.title: Access to CrowdTangle Deletion Framework API

affected_components: GraphQL API endpoint for deletion framework

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access GraphQL endpoints related to the CrowdTangle API and there is a possibility of overly broad data exposure.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to the CrowdTangle platform or its associated APIs.
applicability_signals: Existence of GraphQL endpoints that can be queried.
API documentation or observable responses indicating a 'deletion framework' or similar data retrieval capability.
skip_if: All GraphQL queries return authorization errors.
No GraphQL endpoints related to data deletion or historical data retrieval are found.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify and enumerate accessible GraphQL endpoints within the CrowdTangle API. Look for introspection queries to understand the schema.
Step 2: Analyze the GraphQL schema for queries related to 'deletion', 'history', 'removed items', or 'frameworks'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute a GraphQL query targeting the identified deletion framework or similar data retrieval endpoint. The writeup suggests a root query that grants access to numerous API calls, including one listing deleted objects.
Step 2: Parse the response for fields like '__typename', 'id', 'parent_id', and 'system', which are indicative of deleted objects.
Step 3: Analyze the 'parent_id' to infer associations with specific Facebook pages or accounts, cross-referencing with public data if possible.
Step 4: Attempt to filter or paginate results by date if the API supports it, to download a larger sample of deleted object metadata.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying GraphQL requests)
GraphiQL or similar GraphQL client (for schema exploration and query building)
success_indicators: Receiving a JSON response containing structured data for deleted objects, including 'id', 'parent_id', and 'system'.
The presence of 'CrowdTangleDeletionResult' in the '__typename' field.
Successfully correlating 'parent_id' with known Facebook pages or accounts.
failure_indicators: Receiving GraphQL errors indicating unauthorized access or missing fields.
Responses are empty or do not contain the expected data structure for deleted objects.
All queries result in `null` for relevant fields, suggesting the API is properly secured or no relevant data exists.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of metadata about deleted Facebook posts and associated account information (parent_id). This could potentially reveal which pages or accounts are actively deleting content, or at what scale, which might be sensitive information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize GraphQL API endpoints for root queries that might expose sensitive historical or deletion-related data without proper authorization checks, especially when dealing with platform-specific functionalities like content deletion.

meta.title: Account Takeover (User + Admin) Via Password Reset

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the password reset token is a predictable, sequentially generated number.
prerequisites: Attacker can initiate a password reset for their own account.
Attacker can identify a target user's email address (e.g., from an 'About Us' page).
applicability_signals: Password reset tokens are numeric and appear to increment consecutively.
The password reset endpoint accepts a 'token' parameter.
skip_if: Password reset tokens are UUIDs or cryptographically random.
Password reset tokens are session-bound and cannot be reused or modified externally.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a target user's email address (e.g., from the 'About Us' page).
Step 2: Initiate a password reset for your own account and record the received reset token.
Step 3: Initiate a password reset for the target user's account and record the received reset token. Observe if the token is the next consecutive number.
attack_steps: Step 1: Perform a password reset for your account (Account A) and note the token: `https://dashboard.example.com/password-reset/form?token=<YOUR_TOKEN>`.
Step 2: Perform a password reset for the victim's account (Account B) and note the token: `https://dashboard.example.com/password-reset/form?token=<VICTIM_TOKEN>`.
Step 3: Observe that `<VICTIM_TOKEN>` is `<YOUR_TOKEN> + 1`.
Step 4: Construct a malicious reset link by incrementing your token: `https://dashboard.example.com/password-reset/form?token=<YOUR_TOKEN_PLUS_ONE>`.
Step 5: Use the constructed malicious link to reset the victim's password.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and modify HTTP requests for password reset links.
success_indicators: Ability to log in as the victim user after resetting their password using the manipulated token.
failure_indicators: Received a 400 or similar error when trying to use a modified token, indicating token validation failure. Pivot: Look for other vulnerabilities in the password reset flow.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of any user, including administrative accounts, by predicting and reusing password reset tokens. This allows the attacker to gain unauthorized access, modify user data, or perform actions on behalf of the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that password reset tokens are cryptographically secure, unpredictable, and single-use. Avoid sequential or predictable token generation.

meta.title: Detecting Jackson deserialization vulnerabilities with CodeQL

tech_stack_fingerprints: presence of Jackson Databind library in dependencies

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target application uses the Jackson Databind library and deserializes data originating from an attacker. Additionally, polymorphic type handling must be enabled, and generic types like `Object`, `Serializable`, or `Cloneable` must be in use.
prerequisites: Target application uses Jackson Databind library.
Deserialized data originates from an attacker-controlled source.
Polymorphic type handling is enabled (e.g., `ObjectMapper.enableDefaultTyping()`).
Application uses generic types such as `Object`, `Serializable`, or `Cloneable`.
applicability_signals: Code contains `ObjectMapper mapper = new ObjectMapper();`
Code contains `mapper.enableDefaultTyping();` or equivalent polymorphic type handling configuration.
Code contains `mapper.readValue(data, SomeGenericType.class);` where `SomeGenericType` is `Object`, `Serializable`, `Cloneable`, or other generic types.
The `data` variable originates from an untrusted source (e.g., network socket, HTTP request body).
skip_if: Jackson Databind library is not used.
Deserialization does not involve attacker-controlled data.
Polymorphic type handling is not enabled.
Only primitive or specific non-generic types are deserialized.
A type validator is configured and prevents deserialization attacks.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze source code for usage of `ObjectMapper` and its `readValue` methods.
Step 2: Identify if `enableDefaultTyping()` or equivalent polymorphic type handling is enabled on `ObjectMapper` instances.
Step 3: Trace the origin of data passed to `readValue` to determine if it comes from untrusted sources (e.g., network input, user requests).
Step 4: Check if generic types (`Object`, `Serializable`, `Cloneable`, etc.) are used as the target type for deserialization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a serialized payload containing a gadget that exploits known Jackson deserialization vulnerabilities. This payload should aim to instantiate and execute malicious code when deserialized.

Step 2: Send the crafted payload to the application endpoint that performs the deserialization. This might involve sending it via a network socket, an HTTP request body, or any other data input channel identified in recon.
Step 3: Monitor for signs of successful exploitation, such as unexpected application behavior, exceptions, or callback to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
payloads_or_patterns: Generic Jackson Gadget Payload (Requires specific gadget knowledge based on Jackson version and available libraries, e.g., using `ChainsExploiter` or similar tools to generate payloads that execute arbitrary commands or load classes.)
tools_recommended: CodeQL: To analyze source code for vulnerable patterns.
ysoserial/ysoserial-modified: To generate malicious deserialization payloads for various Java gadget chains.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify network requests, and to host a listener for callbacks (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
success_indicators: Remote Code Execution (RCE) demonstrated via command output or shell access.
Unexpected application behavior or crashes attributed to the deserialized payload.
Callback received from the target system to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) indicating successful gadget chain execution.
failure_indicators: No deserialization exception is thrown, but no malicious behavior is observed (e.g., application continues to run normally). → The gadget chain might be ineffective, or the target type doesn't allow for arbitrary code execution. Try a different gadget.
A `ClassNotFoundException` or `NoClassDefFoundError` is returned. → The required gadget classes are not present in the application's classpath. Need to find a gadget that uses available classes.

An `AccessControlException` or similar security exception is thrown. → JVM security restrictions are preventing the gadget from executing. May require different exploitation techniques or targeting different vulnerabilities.
WAF blocks the request. → Obfuscate payload or use alternative delivery methods.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing an attacker to compromise the application, access sensitive data, perform arbitrary actions, or pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing Java applications using Jackson Databind, always check for the combination of untrusted data deserialization, enabled polymorphic type handling, and the use of generic types, as this is a strong indicator of potential Insecure Deserialization vulnerabilities.

meta.title: How to be popular: CSRF via HTML form POST with JSON type confusion

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target's message sending endpoint accepts POST requests with a JSON-encoded body and does not validate CSRF tokens.
prerequisites: Victim is logged into OkCupid.
Attacker can host a webpage with an HTML form.
applicability_signals: POST requests to /1/apitun/messages/send contain a JSON body.
The /1/apitun/messages/send endpoint does not require a CSRF token.
skip_if: The target application validates CSRF tokens for message sending.
The target application enforces CORS policies that prevent cross-origin form submissions.
All message sending attempts result in errors unrelated to JSON parsing or CSRF.

recon_steps: Observe network traffic for authenticated message sending actions.
Identify the endpoint, HTTP method, and request body format (JSON) used for sending messages.
Verify if a CSRF token is present in the request headers or body.
attack_steps: Create an HTML page containing a form that POSTs to the OkCupid message sending endpoint (/1/apitun/messages/send).
Configure the form's action to the target endpoint.
Use JavaScript to automatically submit the form on page load.
